text: The surgeon applies gentle traction and counter traction to divide areolar tissue using energy sources or cold techniques. As the dissection progresses, the midline preperitoneal fat becomes visible, confirming the correct surgical plane. Care is taken to avoid breaching the PRS and entering the retro muscular plane, which would expose muscle tissue. The surgeon anticipates challenges around the umbilicus and focuses on completing the dissection to maintain the integrity of the peritoneum and avoid creating button holes.
The surgeon continues to create space for the mesh placement by dissecting horizontally near the umbilicus, taking care not to open the peritoneum to avoid complications in subsequent steps.

The surgeon carefully reduces the pre-peritoneal fat herniated into the umbilical defect, revealing the left border of the defect. The fat is repositioned back into the abdominal cavity, and further dissection is performed around the umbilical defect to achieve clearance. The surgeon takes precautions to avoid skin injury or exposure to energy sources during the procedure.

The surgeon performs gentle dissection using short bursts of electrocautery to carefully expand the preperitoneal space, avoiding excessive current to prevent abdominal wall contraction and potential injuries.

The surgeon alternates between the superior and inferior aspects to create a wider plane for mesh placement. External skin markings guide the dissection, with assistance from the surgical team to ensure the plane is adequately sized for the mesh.

The surgeon carefully avoids the inferior epigastric vessels during dissection in the inferior portion to ensure hemostasis. The defect is then closed using a barbed suture, specifically a V-Loc PDS, with attention to not strangulate the tissue while ensuring a snug closure for optimal healing.

The surgeon places the mesh over the defect, securing it with two pre-tied sutures at the twelve o'clock and six o'clock positions for transracial fixation. The mesh is oriented within the plane, which requires minimal fixation due to the collapse of the pneumoperitoneum, ensuring the mesh remains in place.

The surgeon ensures the mesh is flat and well-positioned with two trans fascial sutures in place, checking for proper dissection and absence of corner folding. Additional sutures are placed using Vicryl to minimize postoperative pain. A figure-of-eight technique is employed for challenging areas, using an instrument for retraction. The peritoneal defect is closed with a barbed suture, starting from the outside to keep the knot external. The surgeon uses a parachuting technique, taking multiple loose bites and tightening them simultaneously.

The surgeon repeats the suturing steps to close the peritoneal incision, ensuring a snug closure to prevent internal herniation. The sac is transfixed to prevent fluid collection and herniation, marking the end of the suturing process.
